title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the bicycle. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: Pedal Power: The Evolution of the Bicycle
content.text: The bicycle is often cited as the most efficient form of transport ever devised, yet its development was surprisingly slow and filled with strange diversions. The story begins in 1817 with Baron Karl von Drais, a German inventor who created the 'Laufmaschine' or 'running machine.' It was a wooden frame with two wheels, but it had no pedals. ||(41)|| [41]This awkward 'hobby-horse' was a short-lived fad among the wealthy, but it proved that a two-wheeled vehicle could be balanced by a human[/41]. It wasn't until the 1860s that pedals were finally attached to the front wheel, creating the 'Boneshaker.' As the name suggests, the combination of iron tires and cobblestone streets made for an incredibly uncomfortable ride. ||(42)|| [42]This desire for speed led to the invention of the 'Penny Farthing.' By making the front wheel enormous, engineers ensured that one rotation of the pedals would cover a huge distance[/42]. However, the high center of gravity made it notoriously dangerous; a small pebble could send the rider over the handlebars in a 'header.' ||(43)|| [43]The breakthrough that made cycling a universal activity was the 'Safety Bicycle,' introduced in the 1880s[/43]. By using two wheels of equal size and a chain-driven rear wheel, the rider could sit safely between the wheels while still achieving high speeds. This design is the direct ancestor of every bicycle we see today. ||(44)|| [44]The impact of the safety bicycle was nothing short of a social revolution[/44]. For the first time, people had a cheap, independent way to travel beyond their own villages. This was particularly transformative for women, who gained a new sense of freedom and mobility that challenged the restrictive social norms of the Victorian era. ||(45)|| [45]As the 20th century progressed, the bicycle became more specialized[/45]. The invention of derailleurs allowed riders to change gears, making it possible to conquer steep mountains and long distances. Cycling transformed from a leisure activity into a professional sport, epitomized by the grueling stages of the Tour de France. ||(46)|| [46]Today, the bicycle is experiencing a modern renaissance[/46]. As cities struggle with congestion and pollution, the 'electric bike' is bridging the gap between traditional cycling and motorized transport. The simple machine that began as a wooden frame with no pedals has become a key tool in the fight for a sustainable future.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] to move forward [/41], the rider had to push their feet against the ground in a walking motion. While it was faster than walking, it was exhausting to use on hills and offered no mechanical advantage over the traditional horse and carriage.
paragraphs.B: Despite [42] these physical discomforts [/42], the Boneshaker sparked a 'bicycle craze' across Europe and America. It became a symbol of modern progress, leading inventors to look for ways to make the machines faster and more efficient for the daring young men of the era.
paragraphs.C: This [43] inherent instability [/43] meant that cycling remained the domain of the young and the athletic. It was clear that for the bicycle to become a mass-market product, a design was needed that offered both speed and safety for the average person.
paragraphs.D: The [44] introduction of the pneumatic tire [/44] shortly after was the final piece of the puzzle. By filling rubber tubes with air, John Boyd Dunlop transformed the 'Boneshaker' experience into a smooth, comfortable journey, making long-distance travel a pleasure rather than a chore.
paragraphs.E: This [45] newfound independence [/45] was even credited by some activists with doing more to emancipate women than anything else in the world. It allowed them to leave the domestic sphere without a male chaperone, forever changing the dynamics of the family and the workforce.
paragraphs.F: This [46] technological evolution [/46] has continued into the digital age. Modern frames are made of carbon fiber and titanium, designed using wind tunnels to minimize drag and maximize every watt of energy produced by the rider’s legs.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] early city planners [/47] actually tried to ban bicycles from the streets. They argued that the silent machines frightened horses and posed a danger to pedestrians, a debate that has found a modern echo in the current arguments over bike lanes.

questions.explanation: A explains how the 'running machine' (no pedals) was operated, filling the gap after the introduction of von Drais's invention.
B refers back to the 'physical discomforts' of the 'Boneshaker' and leads into the desire for more speed mentioned after the gap.
C refers to the 'inherent instability' of the Penny Farthing and explains why a safer design (the Safety Bicycle) was necessary.
D introduces the pneumatic tire as the 'final piece of the puzzle' that completed the transition from the Boneshaker to the modern smooth ride.
E expands on the 'newfound independence' for women, providing specific social context for the 'social revolution' mentioned in the text.
F links the 'technological evolution' of gears and professional sport to the modern use of advanced materials like carbon fiber.
